content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Tom ___ (study) harder for his exam last month, he would have passed without any problems.
distractor_rationale: would have in the if-clause is a classic learner error; the if-clause requires past perfect, not would have
simple past in the if-clause is incorrect for third conditional; past perfect is required to refer to an unreal past situation
present perfect cannot be used to describe a completed past event set in last month

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If she had set her alarm clock last night, she ___ (miss, negative) her morning flight to the conference.
distractor_rationale: would not miss is second conditional form and refers to an unreal present situation, not an unreal past event
had not missed places the result clause in past perfect, which is only used in the if-clause of a third conditional
did not miss uses simple past, which describes a real past event rather than a hypothetical past result

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (arrive) at the restaurant on time last Saturday if the bus had not been delayed by the roadworks.
distractor_rationale: would of is a spelling error based on the spoken contraction would've; of is never used as an auxiliary verb
had arrived places the result in past perfect, which belongs in the if-clause, not the result clause
would arrive is second conditional form and refers to an unreal present, not an unreal past situation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the weather ___ (be) better during our camping trip last summer, we would have enjoyed it much more.
distractor_rationale: would have been in the if-clause is a common learner error; the if-clause requires past perfect, not would have
was uses simple past, which is used in second conditional for unreal present situations, not third conditional for unreal past
were is the subjunctive form used in second conditional if-clauses, not in third conditional which requires past perfect

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Mark would have got the job last spring if he ___ (prepare) better for the interview and brought more examples of his work.
distractor_rationale: would have prepared in the if-clause is a frequent learner error; only the result clause takes would have, not the if-clause
prepared uses simple past, which applies to second conditional for unreal present, not to third conditional for unreal past
has prepared uses present perfect, which cannot refer to a specific completed past event in last spring

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Anna had taken the doctor's advice seriously two years ago, she ___ (recover) from her illness much faster.
distractor_rationale: would of recovered is a spelling error; of cannot function as an auxiliary verb in place of have
would recover is the second conditional result form for unreal present situations, not for unreal past events
had recovered is past perfect, which belongs in the if-clause of a third conditional, not in the result clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the team ___ (follow) the coach's plan during the match last weekend, they would have won the championship.
distractor_rationale: would have followed in the if-clause is a typical learner error; the if-clause of a third conditional requires past perfect, not would have
followed is simple past, which is used in second conditional if-clauses for unreal present, not for unreal past events
were following is past continuous, which describes an ongoing action in the past and cannot form the hypothetical past condition required here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David ___ (not, spend) so much money on that old car last year if he had known it would break down after just two weeks.
distractor_rationale: would not spend is second conditional form and refers to a hypothetical present situation, not a hypothetical past result
had not spent is past perfect, which is the form used in the if-clause, not in the result clause of a third conditional
did not spend uses simple past, which describes a real past action rather than a hypothetical past result

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Lisa ___ (tell) her manager about the problem earlier in the project, the whole team would have avoided those costly delays.
distractor_rationale: would have told in the if-clause is a common learner error; the if-clause requires past perfect had told, not would have
told is simple past, which forms the if-clause of a second conditional referring to an unreal present, not a third conditional for unreal past
has told is present perfect and cannot be used to refer to a specific hypothetical past event within a completed project

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children ___ (enjoy) the school trip to the science museum last Friday much more if the guide had explained things in a simpler way.
distractor_rationale: would of enjoyed is a spelling error caused by mishearing the contraction would've; of is not an auxiliary verb
would enjoy is the result clause form for a second conditional referring to an unreal present situation, not an unreal past event
had enjoyed is past perfect, which is the form used in the if-clause of a third conditional, not in the result clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Tom had studied harder for the exam, he ___ (pass) it and started his new job on time.
distractor_rationale: the past participle 'passed' is required after 'would have'; omitting it produces an ungrammatical form
'would of' is a spelling error based on mishearing the contracted form 'would've'; 'of' is never an auxiliary verb
'had passed' is the past perfect form used in the if-clause condition, not in the result clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If she ___ (leave) home ten minutes earlier, she would have caught the last bus to the station.
distractor_rationale: using 'would have' in the if-clause is the most common third conditional error; 'would have' belongs in the result clause, not the condition
simple past 'left' is used in second conditional if-clauses for unreal present situations, not in third conditional if-clauses referring to the past
present perfect 'has left' cannot introduce a hypothetical past condition; past perfect is required

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We would have arrived at the hotel before dark if the driver ___ (take) the correct road at the junction.
distractor_rationale: 'would have taken' incorrectly places the modal result structure inside the if-clause, a typical learner error in third conditional
'took' is simple past, appropriate for second conditional unreal present situations but not for a completed past hypothetical
'has taken' is present perfect and cannot express a hypothetical condition set entirely in the past

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Mark had told his manager about the problem sooner, the team ___ (not lose) so many important clients last month.
distractor_rationale: the past participle 'lost' is required after 'would not have'; using the base form 'lose' is ungrammatical
'would of' is a non-standard misspelling of the contracted 'would've'; 'of' cannot function as an auxiliary
'had not lost' is a past perfect negative, which belongs in the if-clause condition, not in the result clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Anna had saved more money during the year, she ___ (be able to) go on the trip to the coast with her colleagues.
distractor_rationale: 'would be able to' is the result clause form for second conditional referring to an unreal present situation, not for a completed past hypothetical requiring 'would have been able to'
'would of been able to' contains the non-standard 'of' instead of the auxiliary 'have', making it grammatically incorrect
'had been able to' is a past perfect form that belongs in an if-clause condition, not in the result clause of a third conditional

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Tom ___ his alarm, he would have arrived at the meeting on time.
distractor_rationale: using 'would have' in the if-clause is a common error; the if-clause requires past perfect, not the result form
simple past 'set' is used in second conditional for unreal present situations, not for unreal past events
present perfect 'has set' cannot be used in the if-clause of a third conditional referring to a completed past situation

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Maria had studied harder for the exam, she ___ a much better grade.
distractor_rationale: 'would of' is a spelling error based on the contracted spoken form 'would've'; 'of' is never an auxiliary verb
'would receive' is the result clause form for second conditional referring to an unreal present situation, not an unreal past
'had received' is the past perfect form used in the if-clause, not in the result clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We ___ the last bus if we had not stopped to buy snacks at the shop.
distractor_rationale: 'would of caught' is a spelling mistake; 'of' cannot function as an auxiliary verb in English
'would catch' belongs to the second conditional result clause for hypothetical present or future situations, not past
'had caught' is a past perfect form appropriate for the if-clause, not the result clause of a third conditional

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If the weather ___ so cold last weekend, we would have gone to the beach.
distractor_rationale: 'would not have been' incorrectly places the result clause form inside the if-clause; the if-clause requires past perfect
'was not' is simple past, which belongs to the second conditional if-clause for unreal present situations
'has not been' is present perfect and cannot refer to a completed past situation in a third conditional if-clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna would have felt less nervous at the presentation if her manager ___ her more support beforehand.
distractor_rationale: 'would have given' incorrectly uses the result clause structure inside the if-clause; the if-clause must use past perfect
'gave' is simple past, which is used in second conditional if-clauses for unreal present or future situations, not past
'has given' is present perfect and cannot anchor a completed past hypothetical situation in a third conditional

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If James had taken the doctor's advice six months ago, he ___ so tired all the time now.
distractor_rationale: 'would not have felt' is the standard third conditional result form, but the time marker 'now' signals a mixed conditional where the result refers to the present, requiring 'would not feel'
'had not felt' is past perfect and belongs in an if-clause, not in the result clause
'did not feel' is simple past and cannot express a hypothetical present result connected to a past condition

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The project ___ on schedule if the team had communicated better during the first week.
distractor_rationale: 'would of finished' is a spelling error produced by mishearing the contraction 'would've'; 'of' is not an auxiliary
'would finish' is the second conditional result form for unreal present or future situations, not for an unreal past event
'had finished' is past perfect, which is required in the if-clause of a third conditional, not in the result clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Lisa ___ the job offer last spring, she would have moved to a different city by now.
distractor_rationale: 'would have accepted' wrongly places the result clause auxiliary in the if-clause; the if-clause requires past perfect
'accepted' is simple past, which is used in second conditional if-clauses for unreal present scenarios, not for a past hypothetical
'has accepted' is present perfect and cannot express a hypothetical completed past action in a third conditional if-clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If they had booked the hotel earlier, they ___ so much money on last-minute prices.
distractor_rationale: 'would not of spent' is a spelling error; 'of' cannot serve as an auxiliary verb in place of 'have'
'would not spend' is the second conditional result form for hypothetical present or future situations, not for a completed past event
'had not spent' is past perfect, which belongs in the if-clause, not in the result clause of a third conditional

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If David had saved more money during his last job, he ___ able to buy his own flat today.
distractor_rationale: 'would have been' is the standard third conditional result form, but 'today' signals a mixed conditional where the result refers to the present moment, so 'would be' is required
'had been' is past perfect, which is used in the if-clause of a third conditional, not in the result clause
'was' is simple past and cannot express a hypothetical present result linked to an unreal past condition

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.

content.error_sentence: If she had studied harder, she would of passed the final exam last month.
content.corrected_sentence: If she had studied harder, she would have passed the final exam last month.

content.error_sentence: If James had remembered to save his document, he wouldn't lose all his work when the computer crashed.
